# US Army TC 25-20, A Leader's Guide to After-Action Reviews (light reference)

**Source:** Headquarters, Department of the Army (1993). *A Leader's Guide to After-Action Reviews*. Training Circular 25-20, 30 September 1993. Distribution: Approved for public release; distribution unlimited. Licence: **Public domain (17 USC §105 — US Government work product).**
**Structure:** Five chapters plus Appendix A and Glossary. Ch 1 *The After-Action Review* (definition, types, four-step process). Ch 2 *Planning the AAR* (AAR plan, OC selection, stopping points, attendance, sites, training aids). Ch 3 *Preparing for the AAR* (review doctrine, identify key events, observe and take notes, collect from other OCs, organise observations, prepare site, rehearse). Ch 4 *Conducting the AAR* (introduction and rules, review objectives and intent, summary of events, discussion of key issues, optional topics, force protection, closing). Ch 5 *Following Up* (benefits, immediate retraining, revised SOPs, AAR in combat). Appendix A AAR techniques from combat training centers. References: FM 25-100, FM 25-101.

## Thesis (condensed)

The TC's organising claim is that the AAR is the keystone of the Army's training-evaluation process. The AAR is defined as a *professional discussion of an event, focused on performance standards, that enables soldiers to discover for themselves what happened, why it happened, and how to sustain strengths and improve on weaknesses* (Ch 1). The discovery framing is the load-bearing feature — soldiers discover, leaders do not transmit. The text takes a strong position against the critique frame on epistemic grounds: a critique gives one viewpoint, suppresses discussion, focuses only on what is wrong, and stifles learning and team building, whereas an AAR aggregates multiple perspectives and produces stronger recall and behaviour change.

A second load-bearing claim is that the *spirit* and *climate* of the AAR matter as much as the procedure. The Preface frames this directly: the environment must be one in which soldiers and leaders "openly and honestly discuss what actually transpired in sufficient detail and clarity that not only will everyone understand what did and did not occur and why, but most importantly will have a strong desire to seek the opportunity to practice the task again." A third claim is that the AAR is a four-step cycle — Planning, Preparing, Conducting, Following up — not an event. Chapter 5 is explicit that the benefits come from applying results to future training; the cycle closes only when retraining occurs. A fourth claim is that AARs are not graded and do not produce a verdict: there are always weaknesses to improve and strengths to sustain.

## Definition and types of AAR

The AAR is a professional discussion focused on standards. It provides candid insights from multiple perspectives, feedback critical to battle-focused training, and details often lacking in evaluation reports alone. Evaluation is the basis for the commander's unit-training assessment; the AAR is the keystone of that evaluation because no single commander can see what individual soldiers and leaders see during training.

All AARs follow the same general format. The distinction between formal and informal AARs is in resource intensity and preparation, not in the underlying method. Formal AARs are planned six to eight weeks in advance, usually held at company level and above, and may involve site reconnaissance, training-aid coordination, and OC selection and training. Informal AARs are conducted for soldier and small-unit training at platoon level and below, can use minimal aids (the TC's worked example uses pinecones), and provide immediate feedback while training is still fresh. Squad and platoon AARs are held before higher-echelon formal AARs so that lower-level observations can feed upward.

## Planning the AAR

The AAR plan is the foundation: who will observe and lead, what trainers should evaluate (via training-and-evaluation outlines extracted from ARTEP mission training plans), who attends, when and where the AAR occurs, what training aids will be used. External evaluators come from the headquarters two levels above the unit being evaluated (division evaluates battalion, brigade evaluates companies, etc.).

Observer-and-controller (OC) selection prioritises experience over rank: when in conflict, trainers should choose experience and current-TTP knowledge over rank. A staff sergeant with platoon-sergeant experience can observe a platoon better than a sergeant first class without that experience. OCs should not have competing duties; when they must, leaders in the chain of command evaluate their subordinate units. OC training is itself load-bearing: apprenticeship (an experienced OC assists an inexperienced one), classroom instruction on small-group discussion techniques, and observation of properly conducted AARs are all specified.

Stopping points are scheduled at the end of each critical phase or major training event. Planning durations: platoon AAR 30–45 minutes; company AAR about one hour; battalion and above about two hours. Soldiers receive better feedback and remember lessons longer when the AAR is not rushed; reviewers must address key learning points fully but must not waste time on dead-end issues. Attendance: at squad and platoon levels, everyone attends; at company and higher, often only key players (friendly and OPFOR commanders, unit leaders, FIST chief, RTO, and so on).

Sites should be at or near the training exercise site, ideally allowing soldiers to see the terrain where the exercise took place. Soldier comfort is operational: helmets off when appropriate, shelter from the elements, refreshments, participants not facing into the sun, key leaders up front, vehicle parking and equipment security far enough away to prevent distractions. Training aids are selected by usefulness, not by impressiveness: the bottom line is to use an aid only if it makes the AAR better. The TC distinguishes formal AAR aids (terrain model, enlarged map, video camera and monitor) from informal AAR aids (unit markers, rocks and twigs, colored chalk).

## Preparing for the AAR

OCs review current doctrine, technical information, applicable unit SOPs, and the unit's training objectives, orders (OPORDs and FRAGOs), and METL before training. They identify critical events to observe: issuance of OPORDs and FRAGOs, troop-leading procedures, contact with opposing forces, resupply and reconstitution, intelligence preparation of the battlefield, and passage of lines.

Observation discipline: every observation gets a date-time group, location, players involved, an interpretation tied to task and standard, conclusions, and recommendations (the TC includes a sample worksheet in Figure 3-1). Recording medium is the OC's choice — notebook, prepared forms, 3-by-5 cards, or portable tape recorder — but must be reliable, sufficiently detailed, and consistent. The TC warns against tool fetishism: "he should not get carried away with gadgets when a pencil and paper would do." OC positioning is itself a skill — close enough to see, far enough not to disrupt, professional and low-key throughout. Communications nets and computer traffic (Maneuver Control System) can be monitored as additional observation channels.

The AAR leader needs a complete picture and so collects observations from subordinate OCs, combat-support and combat-service-support units, adjacent units, and the OPFOR. The OPFOR perspective is explicitly included because the enemy's view often clarifies why a unit was or was not successful; in formal AARs, the OPFOR commander briefs his plan and intent to set the stage for discussion.

The leader puts notes in chronological sequence, selects key events by relevance to training objectives, and identifies discussion and teaching points. Site setup uses a horseshoe seating arrangement to encourage discussion and ensure everyone can see. The leader rehearses at the site before announcing the AAR start time and location, allowing OCs to prepare while unit leaders account for personnel and equipment.

## Conducting the AAR

The AAR opens with an attention-getter (joke, anecdote, or historical example tied to the training) followed by a review of purpose and sequence. The introduction establishes three rules: the AAR is a dynamic, candid, professional discussion focused on Army standards; the AAR is not a critique (no single person, regardless of rank, has all the information); the AAR does not grade success or failure.

The recommended sequence: introduction and rules → review of objectives and intent (training objectives; commander's mission and intent; OPFOR commander's mission and intent; relevant doctrine and TTPs) → summary of recent events → discussion of key issues → discussion of optional topics → discussion of force protection → closing comments.

The leader uses open-ended and leading questions rather than yes/no questions. The TC's worked contrast: ask *"SGT Johnson, what happened when your Bradley crested the hill?"* rather than *"SGT Johnson, why didn't you engage the enemy tanks to your front?"* The first invites narrative; the second invites self-defence. The leader's role is to guide, not to tell soldiers what was good or bad — but must still ensure specific issues, positive and negative, are surfaced.

The discussion can be organised by one of three techniques: chronological order of events (logical, easy to follow, makes effects visible across units); battlefield operating systems (the seven BOS: intelligence, manoeuvre, fire support, mobility/countermobility/survivability, air defence, combat service support, command and control — useful for staff training but with a discipline risk of losing the big picture); or key events/themes/issues (tight focus on critical training events; effective when time is limited). The leader can switch between techniques during a single AAR when systemic issues emerge.

Fratricide is treated as a mandatory deep-discussion topic: all incidents and near-incidents are discussed in detail, regardless of training-versus-combat environment, focused on cause identification and SOP/TTP development to prevent recurrence. Force protection (safety) is specifically addressed in every AAR. Optional discussion topics include soldier and leader skills (often best in a separate AAR led by a unit member), tasks to sustain or improve (with the directive form *train to weakness, not to strength*), statistics (used judiciously to identify trends and reinforce teaching points but not to grade), and others (TLPs, terrain use, synchronisation, etc.).

During the closing summary, the leader reviews and summarises key points, ends on a positive note linking conclusions to future training, then leaves the area so soldiers can discuss the training in private.

## Following up

Benefits come only from applying AAR results to future training. Immediately or shortly after the training event, leaders conduct a *trained-practiced-untrained* (T-P-U) assessment and develop a future-training concept. Retraining should not be delayed except when absolutely necessary; soldiers should understand they did not perform to standard and that retraining will occur. The unit must retrain on tasks not performed to standard before moving to the next training event, and must retrain on critical gate tasks (derived from the Combined Arms Training Strategy) before progressing to more complex tasks.

The AAR may reveal SOP problems; if so, unit leaders revise the SOP and ensure implementation during future training. In combat, the AAR is one of the most effective techniques available: it takes little time, can be conducted almost anywhere consistent with unit security, helps overcome the steep learning curve of units exposed to combat, and reduces repeat mistakes.

## AAR techniques from combat training centers (Appendix A)

The Appendix consolidates field-proven techniques from CTC OCs. Site selection: choose where free exchange of ideas can occur, ideally overlooking areas of significant action (e.g. a hill overlooking an obstacle breach). Site improvement: use camouflage net or tarp against direct sun, vehicle as windbreak, alternate site (building, bunker, range tower, tent) in severe weather. The leader weighs cost-benefit: moving away from a terrain view that allows a good AAR is sometimes the prudent decision.

Training aids by application: tape recorder (radio traffic, OPORDs); video tape recorder and TV monitor (record key events for playback); dry-erase board (portable chalkboard); butcher paper (outlines, teaching points, agenda); map overlay (bigger than 1:50,000 if possible); terrain model (greater detail than a map); model vehicles and toy soldiers (simulate on terrain models). Aids need not be complicated to be effective — a poncho or the side of a tracked vehicle can substitute for butcher paper or a dry-erase board.

Site organisation: discussion leader centrally located, facing the majority, elevated for large groups. Seating in a horseshoe, with the leader of the unit undergoing the AAR front and centre, soldiers to left and right, higher-echelon leaders to the flanks and rear. The placement is deliberate — to keep junior soldiers' comments from being pre-empted by senior presence, so the atmosphere is one of open discussion *not stifled by rank consciousness*. Training aids centrally located so the maximum number can see; terrain boards angled; TV monitors out of direct light; butcher-paper charts in block print large enough for everyone.

## Key planning targets

| Item | Value |
|---|---|
| AAR planning lead time (formal) | 6–8 weeks before execution |
| Platoon-level AAR duration | 30–45 minutes |
| Company-level AAR duration | about 1 hour |
| Battalion and above AAR duration | about 2 hours |
| Seating arrangement | Horseshoe |
| External evaluator distance from unit being evaluated | Two echelons above |
| Map-overlay scale (when used) | Bigger than 1:50,000 if possible |
| Number of BOS in the seven-BOS frame | 7 (INTEL, MVR, FS, M/C/S, AD, CSS, C²) |
| Discussion-organisation techniques | 3 (chronological / BOS / key events) |
| AAR-process steps | 4 (Planning, Preparing, Conducting, Following up) |
| Post-training assessment | T-P-U (Trained / Practiced / Untrained) |
| Mandatory deep-discussion topic | Fratricide (all incidents and near-incidents) |
| Mandatory mention every AAR | Force protection (safety) |

## Key connections

- **FM 25-100, *Training the Force* (November 1988).** Parent doctrine for training planning, including the requirement to schedule retraining as a normal part of planning.
- **FM 25-101, *Battle Focused Training* (September 1990).** The doctrine this TC supplements and expands; Appendix G of FM 25-101 is referenced for additional AAR detail.
- **ARTEP mission training plans (AMTPs) and soldier's manuals.** Source of the training-and-evaluation outlines (TEOs) that specify tasks, conditions, and standards.
- **Combined Arms Training Strategy (CATS).** Source from which critical gate tasks are derived.
- **Combat training centers (CTCs).** Source of the professional AAR practice distilled in Appendix A.

## Signature contrarian positions

1. **The AAR is not a critique.** A critique gives one viewpoint and stifles learning; an AAR aggregates perspectives and produces stronger behaviour change.
2. **The AAR does not grade success or failure.** "There are always weaknesses to improve and strengths to sustain."
3. **Discovery, not transmission, is the mechanism.** Soldiers discover what happened and why; leaders guide but do not lecture or tell.
4. **No yes/no questions.** Open-ended and leading questions only; the TC's Bradley-versus-engage worked contrast makes the rule concrete.
5. **Train to weakness, not to strength.** Retraining focuses on mission-essential tasks the unit did not perform to standard, not on tasks where proficiency is already established.
6. **Statistics are a double-edged sword.** Useful to identify trends; degrading when used to grade. "Chart after chart of ratios, bar graphs, and tables quickly obscures any meaning."
7. **Rank-suppressed seating is a design choice.** Higher-echelon leaders are deliberately seated to the flanks and rear of the horseshoe so junior soldiers' comments are valued equally.
8. **Spirit and climate are load-bearing, not soft.** The Preface frames the climate of the AAR as the necessary condition for the procedure to work.
9. **Experience beats rank in OC selection.** When choosing between experience-in-the-role and rank, choose experience.
10. **AARs are not cure-alls.** Leaders must still make on-the-spot corrections and take responsibility for training; the AAR is one tool among several.
11. **Pencil-and-paper beats gadgetry.** Tools are chosen by fit-to-purpose, not by sophistication.
12. **The benefit lives in the follow-up.** "The real benefits of AARs come from taking the results and applying them to future training" — the cycle closes only when retraining occurs.
13. **The AAR works in combat.** It takes little time, can be conducted almost anywhere, and helps overcome the steep learning curve of units exposed to combat.
